title: Avoid These Fish at All Costs

chunks.title: Introduction to Salmon Shopping at Costco
chunks.summary: The speaker introduces the topic of buying salmon at Costco, highlighting the importance of understanding what types of salmon are available and the common misconceptions about them. He emphasizes the risks of buying farm-raised fish due to contaminants and misleading labeling.
chunks.transcript: All right, guys. I'm here at Costco in Miami. I'm kind of digging this whole Miami life, so we'll see. But I'm not digging the fish in Costco in Miami. They definitely didn't catch it fresh. Come on inside Costco with me. I'm going to show you how to shop for salmon and some other fish at Costco. There's a lot of misleading stuff with salmon. You guys may have heard about dyes and colorings and carbon monoxide and misleading things, but I'm going to break it all down for you. If you don't understand this stuff, you're going to end up buying the wrong type of fish at Costco. You're going to end up paying more. And you might even find out they're charging you extra at Costco to take the antibiotics out of your farmra salmon. So, come on, let's go. And I

chunks.title: Types of Salmon: Wild vs Farm-Raised
chunks.summary: The speaker discusses the two main types of salmon found at Costco, specifically focusing on Atlantic salmon, which is predominantly farm-raised. He explains that wild salmon gets its color from natural sources, while farm-raised salmon often requires color additives, leading to concerns about nutrition and contamination from antibiotics.
chunks.transcript: comment about this. My phone is always on airplane mode. It's always on airplane mode, even when it's in my back pocket. All right, guys. So, there are two main types of salmon at Costco. The most common type of salmon here is Atlantic salmon. There is no wild Atlantic salmon ever, essentially ever, unless it's line caught. You might be able to go on a boat and catch a wild salmon in the Atlantic Ocean, but anytime you see Atlantic salmon on a label, it is farm-raised. And you'll see on all of these Atlantic salmon labels that farmraised means color added. Because a farm-raised salmon is not getting its color from the food it's eating. Wild salmon gets its color from krill and there's a pigment in krill aazanthin that acts as an antioxidant. It's probably good for humans. That's what gives the wild salmon its rich orange color. Different types of wild salmon have more color. Sakai salmon is the most red salmon that has this deep red orange color. It eats a lot of krill and you get a lot of aazanthin and sckeye. Coo is in the middle. It's a little more orange. And then king salmon sometimes is even white or just light orange because it eats less of these. King salmon actually has an enzyme that present prevents it from metabolizing asanthin. King salmon is my favorite when it's wild. But this Atlantic salmon doesn't look like this color unless it's fed betaarotene in the feed. So what is the problem with Atlantic salmon? What is the problem with far farmraised salmon? They're raised in pens or open nets in the ocean. All of those salmon clustered together mean that they have lots of pollution associated with them. They're all together. They share diseases. They have to be fed antibiotics. They're fed pellets of food which are contaminated with pesticides, heavy metals. They get illnesses. They're pooping all over each other. They're eating each other's poop. It's just it's a very festering, unsafe, unclean place to raise salmon. If they're in a net in the ocean, it actually pollutes the ocean around where the net is. This is a process called utroofication where those salmon in the net actually pollutes the ocean around them. So farm raising of salmon is not good for the ocean. It's not good for the environment and it's not good for the uh it's not good for the salmon themselves. So this farm-raised salmon is much higher in things like PCBs, polycllorinated by fennels. It's also higher in PFAS. Those are forever chemicals. It's probably going to be higher in heavy metals because of the way it's raised. So I would never ever ever eat a piece of farmraised salmon, guys. And I need to tell you a secret about farm-raised salmon. I'm in Miami right now and I've been to a couple of restaurants here recently and you'll see something on the menu. I'm going to really nice restaurants. It's usually $50 or $60 for a piece of Pharaoh Island salmon. That's farm-raised Atlantic salmon from the Pharaoh Islands by Norway. It's just a fancy way of saying farmraised salmon. Total BS, guys. Do not buy Pharaoh Island salmon at a restaurant. That's not what you want. But so fresh Atlantic salmon, no good. Do not buy farmra salmon. Do not be misled by Atlantic salmon. chunks.title: Health Risks of Farm-Raised Salmon
chunks.summary: The discussion highlights the environmental and health issues associated with farm-raised salmon, including the use of antibiotics, high contaminant levels (like PCBs and heavy metals), and negative impacts on the ocean ecosystem. The speaker warns against the potential health effects of consuming these contaminants.
